title: What Could Go Wrong?
subtitle: Foundations: the four sources of operational risk

hook.metaphor: Run a busy restaurant kitchen for one night and you'll understand operational risk in your bones. A chef burns himself, the fridge dies, an order ticket gets lost, a water main bursts outside. None of these are about *markets* or *lending* — they're about the messy business of *getting things done*. That's operational risk: everything that can go wrong inside the machine.
hook.scenario: A bank loses millions — not because borrowers defaulted, and not because markets moved, but because a single botched software update took payments offline for a day. Which kind of risk just bit them?

learn: **Operational risk is the risk of loss from inadequate or failed internal processes, people, and systems, or from external events.** That definition (the global Basel one) has four neat sources worth memorising: **people, process, systems, and external events.** Almost every operational mishap is one of these four.

It's the odd one out among risk types. **Credit risk** is a borrower not paying; **market risk** is prices moving. Operational risk is different — it's not about making a bet that goes wrong, it's about the *plumbing* failing while you do everything else. You can't avoid it by not lending; you carry it just by operating.

It hides in plain sight and comes in many flavours: a staff member's honest mistake, a deliberate internal fraud, a broken process, an IT outage, a cyber-attack, a pandemic, a key supplier collapsing. Even **model risk** — a flawed model giving bad answers (remember the biased credit model?) — is operational risk.

Regulators take it very seriously precisely because it's everywhere. Australia's **APRA CPS 230** (Operational Risk Management) requires regulated firms to identify, manage, and build resilience against exactly these failures; New Zealand's RBNZ and India's RBI have parallel expectations, and Basel sets the global tone. The message: operating is itself a risk you must manage.
bridge: So at work, you can instantly categorise an incident by asking 'which of the four?' — was it **people**, **process**, **systems**, or an **external event**? That single question routes a problem to the right owner and the right fix. A people problem needs training or culture; a systems problem needs engineering; you don't solve a process gap with a pep talk.

interactions.title: Which source is it?
interactions.prompt: Sort each mishap into the operational-risk source it springs from.

items.label: A clerk accidentally pays a supplier twice
There's no step to double-check large payments
A server outage halts all transactions
A flood shuts the head office for a week
An employee deliberately siphons off funds
A key cloud provider suffers a global outage

interactions.title: Name that risk
interactions.prompt: The bank lost millions from a botched software update that took payments offline. The cleanest classification is…

options.label: Credit risk — money was lost
options.feedback: Losing money doesn't make it credit risk. No borrower defaulted. The loss came from a failed internal *system change* — that's operational risk.

options.label: Operational risk — a systems/process failure
options.feedback: Exactly. A failed software update is the 'systems' (and arguably 'process') source of operational risk. The plumbing broke.

options.label: Market risk — it affected the bank's value
options.feedback: Market risk is about prices (rates, FX, equities) moving. Nothing here was a market move; it was an internal failure.

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Operational risk is everything that can go wrong while you *operate* — down to people, process, systems, or the outside world — not the bets you make, but the plumbing that runs them.”

checkpoint.q: The four classic sources of operational risk are…
options: People, process, systems, and external events
checkpoint.explain: The Basel definition: loss from failed/inadequate people, processes, and systems, or from external events.

checkpoint.q: What makes operational risk different from credit and market risk?
options: It comes from the business operating, not from a bet on borrowers or prices
checkpoint.explain: It's the plumbing failing while you operate — you carry it just by running, regardless of any lending or trading bet.

checkpoint.q: A flawed model that produces bad decisions is an example of…
checkpoint.explain: Model risk — a model being wrong, biased, or misused — sits under operational risk.
